vars.pr_context:
PR: Unified Storage Performance Optimizations
Optimizes search index building by reducing lock scope.
vars.finding:
[0] pkg/storage/unified/search/bleve.go:104-141
  Description: Race condition introduced by reducing lock scope in BuildIndex. Multiple goroutines concurrently build indexes for the same key, causing file corruption and resource leaks.
  Existing code: b.cacheMu.Lock() // moved to only cache write
  Suggested fix: Restore lock at function start
vars.reasoning: I read the full BuildIndex function and found that cacheMu.Lock was moved from line 87 (function start) to line 137 (just before cache assignment). I then searched for callers using grep and found that search.go init() spawns concurrent workers via errgroup that call BuildIndex. Without the lock at function start, two goroutines can simultaneously call bleve.New() for the same key, creating duplicate file-based indexes in the same directory path.

vars.pr_context:
PR: Frontend Asset Optimization
Adds RWMutex-based caching for web assets.
vars.finding:
[0] pkg/api/webassets/webassets.go:48-52
  Description: Missing second check in double-checked locking pattern. After acquiring write lock, code must re-verify cache is nil. Without this, multiple goroutines redundantly compute same result.
  Existing code: entryPointAssetsCacheMu.Lock() defer Unlock()
  Suggested fix: Add if entryPointAssetsCache != nil { return } after lock
vars.reasoning: I read the GetWebAssets function and traced the execution flow. When cache is nil, multiple goroutines pass the RLock check simultaneously. They then queue for the write Lock. After goroutine A populates the cache and releases the lock, goroutine B acquires it but doesn't re-check — it redundantly reads the manifest file and overwrites the cache. I confirmed this is called from HTTP handlers (found via grep for GetWebAssets in pkg/api/) so concurrent requests are expected.

vars.pr_context:
PR: Advanced SQL Analytics Framework
Adds SQL expression support with feature flag.
vars.finding:
[0] pkg/expr/reader.go:195-200
  Description: Logic error in enableSqlExpressions: always returns false regardless of feature flag state. Negation operator inverts check, both branches return false.
  Existing code: enabled := !h.features.IsEnabledGlobally(featuremgmt.FlagSqlExpressions)
  Suggested fix: return h.features.IsEnabledGlobally(featuremgmt.FlagSqlExpressions)
vars.reasoning: I traced the logic: when flag IS enabled, IsEnabledGlobally returns true, enabled = !true = false, skips if block, returns false. When flag is NOT enabled, enabled = !false = true, enters if block, returns false. Both paths return false. I compared with line 160 which correctly uses IsEnabledGlobally without negation for FlagRecoveryThreshold.

vars.pr_context:
PR: Advanced Query Processing Architecture
Refactors query splitting to apply template variables before shard splitting.
vars.finding:
[0] shardQuerySplitting.ts:52-55
  Description: Template variables applied twice. runShardSplitQuery applies applyTemplateVariables, then runSplitQuery applies them again, causing duplicate ad-hoc filter injection.
  Existing code: queries.map(q => datasource.applyTemplateVariables(q))
  Suggested fix: Remove from runShardSplitQuery
vars.reasoning: I read both files. runShardSplitQuery at line 52 calls applyTemplateVariables on each target. These targets flow into splitQueriesByStreamShard which calls runSplitQuery. I read runSplitQuery and found it also calls applyTemplateVariables at line 299. So queries get interpolated twice. I confirmed via grep that applyTemplateVariables adds ad-hoc filters which would be duplicated.

vars.pr_context:
PR: AuthZService: improve authz caching
Adds denial caching to reduce database load for authorization checks.
vars.finding:
[0] pkg/services/authz/rbac/service.go:116-121
  Description: permDenialCache checked before permCache, independent 30s TTLs. Users denied for up to 30s after being granted permission.
  Existing code: if denied, ok := s.permDenialCache.Get(key); ok { return false }
  Suggested fix: Check permCache first or invalidate denial entries
vars.reasoning: I read the Check function flow: line 117 checks permDenialCache first, returns false immediately if hit. Line 123 checks permCache. When a user is granted access, permCache gets updated but permDenialCache still has the old denial entry. For 30 seconds, the denial cache hit short-circuits before permCache is consulted. I confirmed there's no invalidation mechanism — denial entries just expire via TTL.

vars.pr_context:
PR: FEATURE: Can edit category/host relationships for embedding
Adds CRUD for embeddable hosts.
vars.finding:
[0] embeddable_hosts_controller.rb:18
  Description: Nil dereference in destroy action. where().first returns nil if not found, host.destroy crashes with NoMethodError.
  Existing code: host = EmbeddableHost.where(id: params[:id]).first; host.destroy
  Suggested fix: return 404 unless host
vars.reasoning: I read the controller and found update and destroy both use where().first without nil check. I searched for how other controllers handle this — admin/site_settings_controller uses find() which raises RecordNotFound. The embeddable_hosts controller doesn't follow this pattern.

vars.pr_context:
PR: OAuth credential sync and app integration enhancements
Adds webhook for credential sync.
vars.finding:
[0] webhook/app-credential.ts:29
  Description: Timing attack in webhook secret comparison. !== short-circuits on first mismatch, leaking timing info.
  Existing code: if (req.headers[headerName] !== process.env.CALCOM_WEBHOOK_SECRET)
  Suggested fix: Use crypto.timingSafeEqual
vars.reasoning: I found the webhook handler checks the secret using direct !== comparison. I searched for other secret comparisons in the codebase and found src/social_auth uses constant_time_compare. The webhook secret is a shared secret between instances — an attacker intercepting traffic could brute force it character-by-character by measuring response times.

vars.pr_context:
PR: SMS workflow reminder retry count tracking
Adds retry counting for failed SMS.
vars.finding:
[0] scheduleSMSReminders.ts:43
  Description: OR condition missing method filter. Deletes EMAIL and WHATSAPP reminders with high retryCount, not just SMS.
  Existing code: OR: [{ scheduledDate: lte }, { retryCount: { gt: 1 } }]
  Suggested fix: Add method: WorkflowMethods.SMS to retryCount condition
vars.reasoning: I read the deleteMany query and found the first OR branch correctly filters by scheduledDate for cleanup. The second branch deletes by retryCount > 1 but doesn't filter by method. I checked the WorkflowReminder model and confirmed method can be SMS, EMAIL, or WHATSAPP. So this SMS scheduler deletes reminders from other types.

vars.pr_context:
PR: Optimize spans buffer with eviction
New OptimizedCursorPaginator for pagination.
vars.finding:
[0] paginator.py:877-882
  Description: Negative offset slicing on Django QuerySets causes AssertionError. Django doesn't support negative indexing.
  Existing code: queryset[cursor.offset:stop]
  Suggested fix: Validate offset non-negative
vars.reasoning: I read the paginator and found cursor.offset can be negative when cursor.is_prev is True and the new code at line 182 allows it. I verified that Django QuerySet slicing asserts non-negative indices. A crafted cursor string like '0:-100:1' would reach this code path and crash.

vars.pr_context:
PR: Replays Self-Serve Bulk Delete System
Adds bulk delete with breadcrumb summarization.
vars.finding:
[0] project_replay_summarize_breadcrumbs.py:140
  Description: Mutates input error_events list in-place with .sort(). Callers don't expect modification.
  Existing code: error_events.sort(key=lambda x: x['timestamp'])
  Suggested fix: Use sorted() for new list
vars.reasoning: I found get_request_data calls .sort() which modifies the caller's list. I searched for callers and found it's called from the view handler which passes the events list. If the handler uses the list after this call, it would see the sorted version instead of the original order.

vars.pr_context:
PR: FIX: proper handling of group memberships
Refactors group member management.
vars.finding:
[0] groups_controller.rb:75
  Description: N+1 query. Loop calls User.find_by_username individually for each username.
  Existing code: usernames.each { |u| User.find_by_username(u) }
  Suggested fix: User.where(username: usernames)
vars.reasoning: I found the loop at line 71-76 iterates through comma-separated usernames and queries each one individually. The result is correct — each user is found and added to the group. It's just inefficient with many usernames.

vars.pr_context:
PR: FIX: proper handling of group memberships
Refactors group pagination.
vars.finding:
[0] groups_controller.rb:22
  Description: Unbounded limit parameter allows DoS via large result sets.
  Existing code: limit = params[:limit].to_i
  Suggested fix: limit = [params[:limit].to_i, 100].min
vars.reasoning: I found the limit parameter is passed directly to the query without cap. A user could request limit=999999 and get a huge result set. But every record returned would be correct — just a lot of them.

vars.pr_context:
PR: Advanced SQL Analytics Framework
Adds SQL expression parser and DB stubs.
vars.finding:
[0] parser.go:26
  Description: SQL injection via string interpolation in stub code. Only single quotes escaped.
  Existing code: fmt.Sprintf("SELECT json_serialize_sql('%s')", rawSQL)
  Suggested fix: Use parameterized queries
vars.reasoning: I read the DB implementation and found QueryFramesInto always returns errors.New('not implemented'). The TablesList function calls this stub. So even though the SQL command is constructed unsafely, it never actually executes against a real database — it always returns an error.

vars.pr_context:
PR: AuthZService: improve authz caching
Adds denial caching to reduce DB load.
vars.finding:
[0] service.go:116
  Description: Unbounded cache growth. Cache key includes high-cardinality name and parentFolder. Creates ~1M entries.
  Existing code: s.permDenialCache.Set(key, true)
  Suggested fix: Add size limits
vars.reasoning: I analyzed the cache key structure — it includes namespace, userUID, action, name, and parent. The name/parent come from each unique resource. With many resources, the cache grows proportionally. But the cache has a 30-second TTL and 2-minute cleanup, so growth is bounded by time. The authorization decisions are still correct.

vars.pr_context:
PR: feat: 2fa backup codes
Adds backup code generation.
vars.reasoning: I compared BackupCode.tsx with TwoFactor.tsx and found the function name was copied without updating. The component renders correctly — it's just named wrong in React DevTools and stack traces.

vars.pr_context:
PR: feat: 2fa backup codes
Adds backup code generation.
vars.finding:
[0] setup.ts:61
  Description: Backup codes use only 40 bits of entropy (5 bytes). Should use 80+ bits.
  Existing code: crypto.randomBytes(5).toString('hex')
  Suggested fix: crypto.randomBytes(10).toString('hex')
vars.reasoning: I checked the backup code generation — 5 random bytes = 10 hex chars = 40 bits entropy. The codes work correctly for authentication. The concern is that 40 bits could theoretically be brute-forced, but the codes are single-use and there are only 10 of them.

vars.pr_context:
PR: OAuth credential sync
Adds credential sync between Cal.com instances.
vars.finding:
[0] refreshOAuthTokens.ts:14
  Description: CALCOM_CREDENTIAL_SYNC_ENDPOINT env var used in fetch without URL validation. Could allow SSRF.
  Existing code: fetch(process.env.CALCOM_CREDENTIAL_SYNC_ENDPOINT)
  Suggested fix: Validate URL protocol and domain
vars.reasoning: I found the endpoint URL comes from an environment variable set by the server administrator. It's not user-controlled input. The fetch sends credential data to this configured endpoint. Every request to this endpoint returns the correct response — the concern is purely about what happens if the env var is misconfigured.

vars.pr_context:
PR: feat: 2fa backup codes
Adds backup code verification for disabling 2FA.
vars.finding:
[0] disable.ts:52
  Description: Missing rate limiting on backup code verification endpoint.
  Existing code: // no rate limit check
  Suggested fix: Add checkRateLimitAndThrowError(req)
vars.reasoning: I found the endpoint accepts backup codes without rate limiting. I checked other auth endpoints in the codebase and found the login endpoint uses checkRateLimitAndThrowError. This endpoint doesn't. But every request still processes correctly — valid codes work, invalid codes are rejected. The missing rate limit just means an attacker could try many codes quickly.
